metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC1(e) jq route-auth assertion to aggregate all routes and assert ALL have `authorization_type == "AWS_IAM"`, e.g.: `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route")] | length > 0 and all(.values.authorization_type == "AWS_IAM")`
findings.finding: The AC1(e) jq assertion for route authorization type is incorrect for multi-route APIs: `select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route") | .values.authorization_type == "AWS_IAM"` streams one boolean per matching route; `jq -e` exits 0 if the LAST emitted value is truthy. If two routes exist and the last one lacks AWS_IAM, the assertion passes on an intermediate true and fails on the last false — or vice versa. The intent is to assert ALL routes use AWS_IAM, but the expression only reliably tests the final route in the stream.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1(e)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1(e) expression with: `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route")] | length > 0 and all(.values.authorization_type == "AWS_IAM")`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` is created by this story (it does not exist on main), provide its initial schema (at minimum: what top-level keys surround the per-environment entries, the full shape of each entry beyond `env_id` and `added_at_log_position`), and confirm AC4's 'a test asserts the entry parses' means YAML-loading the file and asserting the specific field values.
findings.finding: .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml does not exist in the repository (.rebar/ contains only cache/) and is not created by any ticket in the depends_on chain (ee0b-c5e2-3454-42f1 and its dependency 8d8e-30bc-6a7f-4cb1). AC4 treats it as a file to be pinned into but does not specify whether this story creates it from scratch, what its full schema looks like (beyond the env_id and added_at_log_position fields mentioned), or what a test asserting 'the entry parses' validates against.
findings.location: AC4 / file_impact entry `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a 'creates this file' note in AC4 and show the minimal schema, e.g.:
```yaml
trusted_environments:
  - env_id: <REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID value>
    public_key: <ssh-ed25519 ...>
    added_at_log_position: <tickets-branch log tip>
```
The test can then assert `yaml.safe_load(path)['trusted_environments'][0]['env_id'] == os.environ['REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Validate the AC1(f) jq path `.lifecycle_meta_arguments.ignore_changes` against the actual `terraform show -json` output for the existing `aws_ssm_parameter.rebar_secrets` resource (which already uses `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`) before committing the assertion, and update the path if it differs.
findings.finding: AC1(f) pins the jq path `.lifecycle_meta_arguments.ignore_changes` in the `configuration.root_module.resources[]` section of `terraform show -json` to assert the Ed25519 key parameter's `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. The Terraform JSON plan output format (versioned schema) does not expose a stable top-level `lifecycle_meta_arguments` key at this location in all Terraform ≥1.10 versions — lifecycle expressions in the configuration section are embedded under expressions, not as a flat `lifecycle_meta_arguments` sibling. If the path is wrong, the jq expression `? // empty | length > 0` silently evaluates to `empty` (falsy), and `jq -e` exits non-zero — making the assertion fail rather than pass, but for the wrong reason (path missing, not lifecycle absent), which will confuse implementers debugging a plan failure.
findings.location: AC1(f)
findings.suggested_fix: Run `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq '.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address | startswith("aws_ssm_parameter"))' | head -50` against the current plan to observe the actual JSON key under which lifecycle settings appear, then update AC1(f) to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the `aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key` resource declaration (alongside the existing `ignore_changes = [value]`), matching the established pattern for irreplaceable stateful resources (`aws_ebs_volume.data`, `aws_eip.gerrit`).
findings.finding: The `aws_ssm_parameter` for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — explicitly described as the sole copy of the Ed25519 trust-anchor private key ('the key exists nowhere else') — has no `prevent_destroy` lifecycle guard. A `terraform destroy` or `terraform apply -target=aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key` would silently delete the only copy of the key, irrecoverably destroying the trust anchor. The plan's own recovery runbook confirms this is non-recoverable (requires minting a new keypair and opening a new era). The existing Terraform already applies `prevent_destroy = true` to the EBS data volume and Elastic IP for the same blast-radius reason.
findings.location: What section / SSM Parameter Store SecureString declaration; AC1 sub-claim (c+d) for the key parameter

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the exact SSM seeding command in the deploy runbook as `aws ssm put-parameter --value fileb://path/to/id_ed25519 --type SecureString --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (using the `fileb://` binary-file protocol) to prevent the private key from appearing as a shell argument in history or /proc/<pid>/cmdline.
findings.finding: The plan's key-seeding step ('generate the key locally with ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "" and seed the SecureString') does not declare the exact `aws ssm put-parameter` invocation form. If the private key is passed as a shell command-line argument (e.g., `--value "$(cat key)"` or inline), it transiently appears in shell history (~/.bash_history), /proc/<pid>/cmdline, and any operator terminal log — all plaintext on the boundary-crossing setup path. The safe form is `aws ssm put-parameter --value fileb://path/to/key` (AWS CLI binary-file protocol), which reads directly from a file descriptor and never exposes the value as a shell argument.
findings.location: What > Deploy + prove section: 'generate the key locally (ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "") and seed the SecureString'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Deploy + prove' runbook, pin the seeding command: `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --type SecureString --value fileb://id_ed25519 [--overwrite]`. The `fileb://` protocol passes the value via file descriptor, never as a shell-expanded argument, closing the shell-history and procfs exposure path. Also note: `shred -u id_ed25519` immediately after seeding.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the error-handling policy for the `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` call in `compose-up.sh`: state whether it should be fatal (exit compose-up on failure, blocking stack start) or non-fatal (WARN + continue, leaving /opcert/ at 403), and align with the rationale for why that policy fits the opcert trust-anchor role.
findings.finding: The plan says `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is wired into `compose-up.sh` 'alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls' but does not specify whether a failure of this script is fatal (exits compose-up.sh) or non-fatal (logs a warning and continues). The two existing precedents have opposite policies: `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is called from `setup-replication.sh` (fatal); `materialize-g2p-config.sh` in compose-up.sh is non-fatal with a WARN. The opcert guard is load-bearing for the nginx origin protection — if the script fails silently, nginx retains a stale or missing guard file and /opcert/ serves 403 indefinitely without any alert, but compose-up.sh proceeds normally.
findings.location: What section — Compose wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh lines 177-179

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the compose-up.sh modification (e.g., a conditional `aws ssm get-parameter … || true` guard in the materialize call, or a documented git-revert step paired with the Terraform destroy sequence) so that a failed or rolled-back deploy does not break subsequent boots of unrelated compose services.
findings.finding: The plan wires `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up`, making every subsequent boot of the existing shared box hard-fail if the SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` does not exist (e.g., before `terraform apply`, in a restored environment, or during a partial rollback). No explicit back-out procedure is given for the compose-up.sh change itself — how to revert the shared boot script if the deploy needs to be undone, or how to make the materialize step non-fatal/conditional to preserve the prior service uptime guarantee.
findings.location: What > Compose section; 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh'
findings.suggested_fix: Make the materialize call in compose-up.sh non-fatal for missing parameters (e.g., `materialize-opcert-guard.sh || { echo 'WARNING: opcert guard not seeded; /opcert/ will serve 403' ; }`) so existing services continue to boot even if the opcert SSM parameter is absent. Document the git-revert of the compose-up.sh change as step 1 of the Terraform rollback procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` within `compose-up.sh`: either (a) treat failure as fatal (set -e / explicit exit-code check) and document that this gates the entire boot, or (b) treat it as non-fatal (opcert stays fail-closed) and emit a visible log/alert so operators know the guard was not materialized — choosing whichever matches the existing precedent for `materialize-deploy-key.sh`.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` failure mode inside `compose-up.sh` is unspecified: if SSM is transiently unavailable at boot, the plan does not state whether compose-up.sh aborts (leaving the whole box in a failed boot) or proceeds (leaving nginx in the fail-closed 403 state with no guard materialized and no operator signal).
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` and `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` checks the exit code of `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (e.g., via `set -e` or an explicit `|| { echo 'ERROR: failed to materialize opcert guard'; exit 1; }`) and aborts the boot sequence on failure, so a transient SSM error produces a loud, obvious failure rather than a silently broken deployment.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` failure path (SSM read denied, EC2 instance metadata unavailable, network timeout, or `aws ssm get-parameter` returning an error) is not specified. While the fail-closed nginx template default (403 on missing guard) is safe, `compose-up.sh` may continue to `docker compose up` even if the script exits non-zero, leaving the operator with an apparently running system that rejects all /opcert/ requests with no obvious error surfaced in the compose log.
findings.location: Compose section — 'infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh'; 'WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh … running BEFORE docker compose up'
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend `set -e` to `compose-up.sh` (if not already present per the existing scripts' convention), or add an explicit check: `materialize-opcert-guard.sh || { echo 'FATAL: opcert guard materialization failed' >&2; exit 1; }`. Document this in the AC3 test assertion for compose-up.sh ordering.

findings.finding: The plan wires `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into `compose-up.sh` to run before `docker compose up`, but does not specify the error-handling behavior if the script exits non-zero at boot time — specifically, whether failure is fatal to the entire compose stack (blocking Gerrit and all other services on the shared VM) or is intentionally non-fatal (leaving /opcert/ in fail-closed 403 state while other services start normally). Given that the stated intent is zero disruption to the existing VM, an unspecified fatal-by-default behavior would contradict it.
findings.location: What section — Compose paragraph: 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh`... running BEFORE `docker compose up`'
findings.suggested_fix: In compose-up.sh, invoke the materialize script with explicit error handling: `materialize-opcert-guard.sh || { echo 'WARN: opcert guard materialize failed; /opcert/ will return 403' ; true; }` — the DENY-ALL default conf (already installed by the nginx-config recipe) keeps /opcert/ fail-closed, while Gerrit and other services start normally. Document this behavior in the boot-ordering notes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf` with mode `0640` (or `0600`) owned by the nginx user/group, so the guard secret is not world-readable on the host filesystem.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script writes the origin-guard value (a shared secret) to `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf` on the host filesystem, but the plan does not specify the file's mode/permissions. If written with the default umask (typically 0022, yielding mode 0644), the guard value is world-readable on the host. While the box is single-tenant with no interactive login users (SSM-only access), any process running on the host (including any compromised container with host-volume access or a future service) could read the guard value and forge direct-to-origin requests, bypassing the API Gateway SigV4 layer.
findings.location: What > Compose / nginx wiring section: 'infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh writes only the one-line map entry /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf'
findings.suggested_fix: In the script: `install -m 0640 -o root -g nginx <(echo '"'"${GUARD_VALUE}"'" 1;') /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf` — or equivalent `printf ... > tmpfile && chmod 0640 tmpfile && mv tmpfile /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf`. This follows the 0600 pattern already applied by `materialize-deploy-key.sh` for SSH private keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) that verifies the key-loss recovery runbook is present in the deploy notes and covers the four recovery steps: generate new keypair, re-seed SSM, add new era entry with `added_at_log_position`, and revoke the lost key's entry with a grandfather pin.
findings.finding: The What section describes a 'Key-loss recovery runbook (documented in the story's deploy notes)' as an explicit deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that this documentation exists, is correct, or covers the stated recovery steps (new keypair, re-seed, new era entry, revocation of old key).
findings.location: What section — 'Key-loss recovery runbook (documented in the story's deploy notes)'; Acceptance Criteria — AC1 through AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 or add AC6: 'The deploy notes include the key-loss recovery runbook covering: (1) generate a new passphrase-free Ed25519 keypair, (2) seed it into `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, (3) add a new era entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with `added_at_log_position` = current tickets tip, and (4) revoke the lost key's entry with a grandfather pin at the loss point — verified by reviewer inspection of the comment/PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and implement) a persistent repo location for the key-loss recovery and guard-rotation runbooks — e.g., a new `infra/docs/opcert-runbook.md` or a clearly marked section in `infra/README.md` — so operators can find these procedures without reading the story.
findings.finding: The key-loss recovery runbook and the guard-rotation runbook are documented only within the plan/story text. The plan does not specify a persistent, operator-discoverable location in the repository where these procedures will live after the story closes (e.g., `infra/README.md`, `docs/runbooks/opcert.md`, or inline comments in the materialize script).
findings.location: What > Deploy + prove section (key-loss recovery); What > Compose section (rotation runbook)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/docs/opcert-runbook.md` covering (1) guard rotation (two ordered steps, the 403 window, expected outcome) and (2) key-loss recovery (new keypair, re-seed, new era entry). Reference it from a comment in `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` and in `compose-up.sh`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new opcert endpoint: specify a CloudWatch log group + API Gateway access logging (`aws_apigatewayv2_stage.access_log_settings`), and at minimum a 5xx-rate alarm so operators can detect silent failures without relying on consumer reports.
findings.finding: No CloudWatch log group, API Gateway access logging, or operational alarm is specified for the new opcert endpoint. Failures (5xx from HOST-nginx, guard rejections with 403, SSM parameter read errors at boot) are invisible to operators.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway and SSM declarations; Compose/nginx wiring
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Terraform declarations: `aws_cloudwatch_log_group.opcert_apigw_logs`, wire it into the HTTP API stage's `access_log_settings.destination_arn`, and add an `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` on `5XXError` for the API. Document the log group name in the deploy notes so operators know where to look.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `nginx -t` config validation before `nginx -s reload` in `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (and document the rollback step as: restore the previous `rebar.conf` from the template backup and reload) so a bad template is caught before it affects the live server.
findings.finding: The `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` change (adding the guard map and /opcert/ location) affects HOST-nginx, which also serves Gerrit and all other services on the box. The plan specifies no nginx config validation step (`nginx -t`) before the reload, and no rollback path if the new template contains a syntax error — a bad template could take Gerrit down.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`; `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`
findings.suggested_fix: In `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`, after writing the guard map file, run `nginx -t` and only proceed to `nginx -s reload` if it exits 0; on failure, log the error and exit non-zero so compose-up.sh (or the operator) knows to investigate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a detection/guard path for the accidental-deletion-then-Terraform-recreation scenario: either a Terraform `precondition` on the key parameter resource asserting the value is non-placeholder after apply (using a `check` block or a `null_resource` with a local-exec validation), or a mandatory startup health check in the compose service that validates the loaded key is a syntactically valid Ed25519 private key before serving any requests — and document this alongside the key-loss recovery runbook.
findings.finding: If the SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is deleted from AWS outside of Terraform (accidental `aws ssm delete-parameter`, account incident, or manual cleanup), `terraform apply` will silently RECREATE the parameter with the placeholder value — `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` suppresses drift on UPDATE but does not prevent a create-on-missing with the placeholder. The compose service would start, load the placeholder as its signing key, and produce invalid or panic-inducing signatures with no obvious error surfaced to operators. The key-loss recovery runbook addresses intentional retirement but not this silent recreation path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString declaration; 'Key-loss recovery runbook' in Deploy + prove section; AC1 sub-claim (c+d)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Terraform `check` block (≥ TF 1.5) or a `null_resource` with `local-exec` that reads the SSM parameter after apply and asserts the value does not equal the placeholder string. Additionally, add a startup assertion in the compose service's entrypoint (e.g., `ssh-keygen -y -f <(echo "$KEY")` exits 0 only for valid keys) so the container refuses to start if the key material is invalid, making the failure loud rather than silent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `hashicorp/random` / `random_password` with the established operator-seeding pattern: generate the origin-guard value out-of-band (`openssl rand -hex 32`) and seed it via `aws ssm put-parameter` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, matching the existing precedent for all 12 SSM secrets in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3), while the codebase's established pattern for every one of its 12+ secrets — including random values — is operator-generated out-of-band then seeded via `aws ssm put-parameter` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. The origin-guard value could be provisioned identically with `aws ssm put-parameter --value "$(openssl rand -hex 32)"` following the existing operator-seeding precedent, without adding a new provider dependency, a new lock-file entry, or Terraform-state serialization of the secret.
findings.location: What section — 'origin-guard header provisioning path'; AC1 — `hashicorp/random` in `required_providers`; `infra/terraform/versions.tf`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the existing `local.rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` (or as a standalone `aws_ssm_parameter` resource with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`). Document the operator seeding step in the deploy notes: `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --type SecureString --value "$(openssl rand -hex 32)"`. Read the value in the Terraform integration via a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` source. Remove the `hashicorp/random` provider from `required_providers`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `nginx -s reload` with `systemctl reload nginx` (or `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` per the template header) in the `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` spec and the rotation runbook, matching the established convention in `install-certbot-timer.sh` and `rebar.conf.template`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `nginx -s reload` for the materialize script and the rotation runbook, while the codebase's established convention — confirmed in two existing call-sites — is `systemctl reload nginx`. This is an unexplained deviation from the existing pattern without justification.
findings.location: What section — 'materialize-opcert-guard.sh … finishes with `nginx -s reload`'; rotation runbook 'then re-run materialize-opcert-guard.sh (rewrites the nginx map + `nginx -s reload`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` — the exact idiom from `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template:10` — so a config-syntax error is caught before the reload.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to `BOT_PATHS` in `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` (line 55) so changes to the opcert Dockerfile trigger automatic service redeploy, matching the `Dockerfile.reviewbot` precedent.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55 defines `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'` — the set of paths that trigger an automatic redeploy. The plan adds `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (from the sibling story) as the opcert build artifact, but neither this plan nor its scope mentions updating `BOT_PATHS`. Changes to `Dockerfile.opcert` would never auto-redeploy the opcert service.
findings.location: What / Compose section; `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55
findings.suggested_fix: Change BOT_PATHS to include 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert' alongside 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a consumer requiring update: add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to `BOT_PATHS` (so opcert source changes trigger a rebuild/restart of the `opcert` compose service) and either add `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to a watch path or document explicitly why auto-refresh of that script is not required.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer of two artifacts this story (and its sibling) introduce. BOT_PATHS (line 55) lists only `src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` — it does NOT include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (sibling story) or `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (this story). Changes to either file after initial deploy will not trigger an auto-redeploy of the opcert service on the box.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55 (BOT_PATHS) / What section (compose wiring)
findings.suggested_fix:
In autodeploy.sh, extend BOT_PATHS or add an OPCERT_PATHS analog:
```bash
OPCERT_PATHS='infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert src/rebar/opcert_service/'
```
and add a `if changed "$OPCERT_PATHS"; then` block that rebuilds/restarts the `opcert` compose service (analogous to the BOT_SERVICE block for review-bot). For materialize-opcert-guard.sh, either include it in CONFIG_PATHS (signalled, manual apply) or document that guard rotation always goes through the Terraform rotation runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` should be added to `BOT_PATHS` (triggers a review-bot redeploy + re-materialize) or handled via `CONFIG_PATHS` (detect-only signal to operator), or document explicitly that day-2 changes to this script require a manual `compose-up.sh` re-run.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is a new file in `infra/scripts/` but is not covered by any of autodeploy.sh's trigger paths (`BOT_PATHS`, `CONFIG_PATHS`, `OBS_PATHS`). A subsequent fix or rotation-procedure update to this script merged to `main` will never be applied to the running box by the auto-deployer. The plan wires it into `compose-up.sh` (boot path) but offers no auto-deploy trigger for day-2 changes.
findings.location: What section — Compose wiring; infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh BOT_PATHS (line 55)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify `hashicorp/random` vs the established operator-seed pattern (all 12 existing secrets) — or switch the origin-guard to operator-seeded (`openssl rand -hex 32` + `aws ssm put-parameter`) and drop the new provider entirely.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` — Rule-of-Three fires (0/3) — while the established codebase pattern for ALL 12 SSM secrets is operator-seeds-a-value-via-`aws ssm put-parameter`. The alternative (operator generates the guard value with `openssl rand -hex 32` and seeds it, exactly like the existing secrets) is not discussed or rejected in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`'; infra/terraform/versions.tf (no hashicorp/random present); infra/terraform/ssm.tf (all 12 existing secrets operator-seeded)
findings.suggested_fix: Declare the guard SSM parameter with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (matching the existing pattern); document that the operator seeds it with `openssl rand -hex 32` before first `terraform apply`. This eliminates the new provider and aligns with the established pattern while keeping the rotation runbook (re-seed SSM + reload nginx + update API Gateway integration header manually or via a Terraform variable).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct `request_parameters` key prefix for unconditional static header injection on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` (AWS docs specify `overwrite:header.<name>` for set-unconditionally semantics) and update both the IaC and the AC1(b) jq assertion accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan specifies injecting the static origin-guard header via `request_parameters` with key `"append:header.X-Opcert-Guard"` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource. The `append:` prefix in AWS API Gateway HTTP API parameter mapping appends to an existing header value rather than overwriting/setting it; the correct prefix for unconditional static injection of a new header is `"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"`. Using `append:` means a caller who already sends `X-Opcert-Guard` will receive a concatenated value at nginx, not the Terraform-controlled value, undermining the guard mechanism.
findings.location: AC1 — sub-claim (b): `has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")` jq assertion; What section: 'injecting a shared origin-guard header' + 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `request_parameters` key to `"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"` in both the Terraform resource and the AC1(b) jq assertion: `has("overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) support resource policies (check `aws_apigatewayv2_api.policy` in the Terraform AWS provider docs) and either correct the claim or document a deliberate decision to rely on identity policy only.
findings.finding: The plan states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the sole architectural rationale for implementing access control via identity policy only (no API-level resource policy). This is an unverified capability assertion: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource policies via the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` `policy` argument in Terraform. If the assertion is wrong, the design foregoes a zero-cost defense-in-depth layer (a resource policy that explicitly denies any principal not in the admin role) and the plan's stated reasoning is internally incorrect.
findings.location: What section: 'an HTTP API has no resource policy': `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4, and the IaC CREATES the admin role it restricts to — … Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` for API Gateway access logs and wire it to the API Gateway HTTP API's default stage via `access_log_settings`, and add a CloudWatch metric alarm (wired to `aws_sns_topic.alerts`) for elevated 4xx or 5xx count on the new API — matching the observability pattern established for every other service in `infra/terraform/monitoring*.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any CloudWatch access logging or error-rate alarming for the new API Gateway HTTP API. Every other internet-facing component in the existing Terraform has a CloudWatch metric alarm wired to the SNS alerts topic (Gerrit reachability, EC2 status checks, disk pressure, replication errors, etc.), but the new API Gateway — the only internet-reachable entry point to the opcert service — has no access log group, no stage-level `access_log_settings`, and no alarm for 4xx/5xx error rates or anomalous invocation counts. Failed SigV4 authentication attempts (401s) and service errors would be invisible without access logging.
findings.location: What section / 'NO new fixed-monthly-cost resources' bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC1 and AC5; no observability sub-criterion in any AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the `request_parameters` key from `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` to `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard` in both the Terraform integration resource and the AC1(b) jq assertion, so the guard header unconditionally overrides any caller-supplied value.
findings.finding: The plan pins `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` as the `request_parameters` key for the API Gateway → origin static guard header (in the What section and locked into AC1(b)), but the `append:` prefix comma-joins the injected value onto any pre-existing header of the same name sent by the caller. For a security guard header that nginx matches exactly (`$http_x_opcert_guard == '<value>'`), a caller who includes their own `X-Opcert-Guard` header would cause API GW to produce a comma-joined value that nginx's exact check rejects — breaking legitimate SigV4-authenticated calls. The correct prefix for unconditionally overriding a header is `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`.
findings.location: What section (API Gateway integration description) and AC1(b)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource: `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`. Update AC1(b) jq to `has("overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim: replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with the accurate reason (e.g., 'identity-policy-only is sufficient because the role trust policy already limits to `opcert_admin_principal_arns`; a resource policy is optional defense-in-depth that is not added to keep the IaC minimal').
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the architectural basis for restricting callers exclusively via an identity policy on the `rebar-opcert-admin` role. AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies (the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` Terraform resource has a `policy` argument; the AWS console exposes it). The stated justification is factually incorrect, which could mislead implementers into believing a defense-in-depth resource policy is architecturally impossible when it is not.
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` itself (its header documents rendering the template via `envsubst` into `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf`; no separate script owns this install today — the recipe IS the owning artifact, and this story edits it as a declared target)' — but a Grep/Read of every script in `infra/scripts/` and `infra/gerrit/` finds NO script that ever calls `envsubst` on `rebar.conf.template` or writes `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf`. The header is a comment block, not an executable artifact. The plan proposes to 'extend it with one more line' as though adding a shell command to an existing script, but the owner script does not exist.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx paragraph: 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template itself … this story edits it as a declared target'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new script `infra/scripts/install-nginx-conf.sh` (or extend `install-certbot-timer.sh`, which already installs host nginx and reloads it) to render the template and write the deny-all default guard conf, then explicitly name this script as the target in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create a new numbered ADR (e.g., `adr-0048-opcert-apigw-origin-guard.md`) capturing the zero-fixed-cost API Gateway + HOST-nginx origin-guard pattern, the rejected Fargate/NLB/VPC-Link alternatives, and the lifecycle ignore_changes convention for the seeded key — or explicitly state that the epic's design resolution doc serves as the canonical ADR and link to it from `infra/terraform/` CLAUDE.md.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel operational pattern (API Gateway HTTP API with AWS_IAM + EC2 HOST-nginx origin-guard as a zero-fixed-cost trust gate) but does not create a new ADR for it — only referencing the existing ADR 0046 and ADR 0047 and the epic's 'design resolution' document, which may not be a durable, numbered architectural record.
findings.location: Why section; What section preamble

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the retirement/decommission path for the SSM parameters: explicitly state that `terraform destroy` (or targeted `terraform destroy -target`) must be run to delete both SecureString parameters when the service is decommissioned, and note that the private key parameter should be force-deleted (`aws ssm delete-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) as part of any service retirement, even if decommission is out of scope for this story.
findings.finding: No retirement/decommission lifecycle is specified for the two SSM SecureString parameters (especially `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, which holds a private key). When the opcert service is eventually decommissioned, the private key material will remain in SSM Parameter Store indefinitely unless explicitly deleted, constituting a persistent credential leak.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store declarations; Scope section ('Out: … resizing or re-homing the existing box')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the `opcert` compose service needs an external named volume; if so, extend `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh` and update the plan — the `config-check.sh` CI gate (lines 91–131) enforces this pairing and will fail the PR if the compose volume is declared `external: true` without a corresponding `SITE_SUBDIRS` entry.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/config-check.sh` (lines 91–131) enforces that EVERY `external: true` volume in `docker-compose.yml` is provisioned by `compose-up.sh` via its `SITE_SUBDIRS` list — a CI gate that exits non-zero on any mismatch. The plan adds an `opcert` compose service but never mentions extending `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh` or the `config-check.sh` enforcement, so if the opcert service uses an external named volume the CI drift gate would fire.
findings.location: What / Compose section; `infra/scripts/config-check.sh` lines 91–131; `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` `SITE_SUBDIRS`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Compose section: 'If the opcert service requires a persistent external volume, add the subdir name to SITE_SUBDIRS in compose-up.sh (config-check.sh check 5 enforces this pairing).'

findings.finding: The plan states the nginx-config install recipe that writes the DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is embedded in `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`'s header as the 'owning artifact', but no script in the repo actually executes `envsubst` on that template — `install-certbot-timer.sh` installs nginx but does not render the template; `compose-up.sh` never references it. The plan does not identify which script is extended to also write the deny-all default guard file at install time.
findings.location: What section — 'Two ordering hazards are closed explicitly: (a) BOOT' / `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
